content.text:
The `.gitignore` file is **untracked** (`?? .gitignore`) — it was never committed, so git has been tracking all your build artifacts (`bin/`, `obj/`, `.dll`, `.pdb`, etc.).

Fix:
1. Add the .gitignore to git
2. Remove the already-tracked build artifacts from the index

```bash
git add .gitignore
git rm -r --cached WebIde.Console/bin/ WebIde.Console/obj/ WebIde.Model/bin/ WebIde.Model/obj/
git commit -m "Add .gitignore and remove tracked build artifacts"
```

After this, those files will be ignored going forward and won't show up as changes.

```bash

```

Then push again:

```bash

```

Using `--rebase` keeps a cleaner history by replaying your commits on top of the remote changes instead of creating a merge commit.

1→# Architecture
     2→
     3→## Overview
     4→
     5→WebIde is a self-hosted competitive coding platform. Users write code in a browser-based editor, submit it, and get results streamed back in real time. The system separates concerns into distinct layers: presentation, business logic, and code execution.
     6→
     7→## Current State
     8→
     9→The domain model layer (`WebIde.Model`) is implemented as a .NET class library. A console app (`WebIde.Console`) exercises the model with LINQ queries and async patterns. No web layer exists yet.
    10→
    11→## Target Architecture
    12→
    13→```
    14→Browser
    15→  └── React/TypeScript SPA
    16→        │
    17→        │ HTTP /api/*
    18→        ▼
    19→  ASP.NET MVC (.NET 10)          ← controllers, services, EF Core
    20→        │
    21→        ├── PostgreSQL            ← persistent storage
    22→        ├── Redis                 ← sessions, caching
    23→        └── Sandbox Worker        ← isolated code execution (Docker)
    24→              │
    25→              │ SignalR
    26→              ▼
    27→        Browser (real-time execution output stream)
    28→```
    29→
    30→Nginx sits in front of everything as a reverse proxy:
    31→- `/app/*` → React frontend (Nginx static in prod, Vite dev server in dev)
    32→- `/api/*` → ASP.NET MVC backend
    33→
    34→## Layers
    35→
    36→### WebIde.Model
    37→Pure C# class library. No dependencies on any framework. Contains:
    38→- Domain entity classes (Problem, Submission, User, etc.)
    39→- Enums (DifficultyLevel, SubmissionStatus, UserRole)
    40→
    41→This library is referenced by any layer that needs to work with the domain — backend API, tests, etc.
    42→
    43→### Backend (planned)
    44→ASP.NET MVC (.NET 10). Responsibilities:
    45→- REST API for the frontend
    46→- Business logic (submission scoring, leaderboard updates)
    47→- EF Core ORM talking to PostgreSQL
    48→- SignalR hub for streaming execution output
    49→
    50→### Frontend (planned)
    51→React + TypeScript, bundled with Vite. Responsibilities:
    52→- Code editor (Monaco Editor)
    53→- Problem browser, submission history, leaderboard
    54→- SignalR client for real-time output
    55→
    56→### Sandbox (planned)
    57→Isolated execution environment for untrusted code. Each submission gets:
    58→- A container with strict CPU, memory, and time limits
    59→- No network access
    60→- Results (stdout, stderr, exit code, wall time, peak memory) written back to the backend
    61→
    62→## Scaling Plan
    63→
    64→| Stage | Users | Infrastructure |
    65→|---|---|---|
    66→| 1 | ~100 | Single VPS (4 vCPU / 8 GB), 2 sandbox runners |
    67→| 2 | ~500 | App node + dedicated worker node, managed PostgreSQL |
    68→| 3+ | 5 000+ | Kubernetes, auto-scaling worker pools, read replicas |
    69→
    70→The bottleneck at scale is the sandbox, not the API or database. C/C++ submissions take ~1s compile + ~2s run, so sandbox runners need dedicated CPU for deterministic timing.
    71→

1→# Domain Model
     2→
     3→All classes live in `WebIde.Model`. The model is a pure class library with no framework dependencies — it can be referenced by any layer.
     4→
     5→## Enums
     6→
     7→### `DifficultyLevel`
     8→Classifies problem difficulty.
     9→```
    10→Easy | Medium | Hard
    11→```
    12→
    13→### `SubmissionStatus`
    14→Tracks the lifecycle of a code submission.
    15→```
    16→Pending → Running → Accepted
    17→                  → WrongAnswer
    18→                  → TimeLimitExceeded
    19→                  → MemoryLimitExceeded
    20→                  → CompileError
    21→```
    22→
    23→### `UserRole`
    24→Controls what a user can do on the platform.
    25→```
    26→Admin | Instructor | Student
    27→```
    28→
    29→---
    30→
    31→## Classes
    32→
    33→### `User`
    34→A registered platform user.
    35→
    36→| Property | Type | Notes |
    37→|---|---|---|
    38→| Id | int | |
    39→| Username | string | required |
    40→| Email | string | required |
    41→| DisplayName | string | required |
    42→| Role | UserRole | |
    43→| RegisteredAt | DateTime | |
    44→| Submissions | List\<Submission\> | 1-N: user has many submissions |
    45→| Organizations | List\<Organization\> | N-N: user belongs to many orgs |
    46→
    47→---
    48→
    49→### `Organization`
    50→A group of users — represents a course, team, or institution.
    51→
    52→| Property | Type | Notes |
    53→|---|---|---|
    54→| Id | int | |
    55→| Name | string | required |
    56→| Description | string | required |
    57→| Members | List\<User\> | N-N with User |
    58→| ProblemSets | List\<ProblemSet\> | 1-N: org owns many problem sets |
    59→
    60→---
    61→
    62→### `Problem`
    63→A coding problem with a description and constraints.
    64→
    65→| Property | Type | Notes |
    66→|---|---|---|
    67→| Id | int | |
    68→| Title | string | required |
    69→| Description | string | required |
    70→| Difficulty | DifficultyLevel | |
    71→| TimeLimitMs | int | execution time cap per submission |
    72→| MemoryLimitKb | int | memory cap per submission |
    73→| CreatedAt | DateTime | |
    74→| AuthorUsername | string | required |
    75→| TestCases | List\<TestCase\> | 1-N: problem has many test cases |
    76→| Tags | List\<Tag\> | N-N: problem has many tags |
    77→| Submissions | List\<Submission\> | 1-N: problem has many submissions |
    78→
    79→---
    80→
    81→### `Tag`
    82→A label for categorizing problems (e.g. "Arrays", "Binary Search").
    83→
    84→| Property | Type | Notes |
    85→|---|---|---|
    86→| Id | int | |
    87→| Name | string | required |
    88→| Problems | List\<Problem\> | N-N with Problem |
    89→
    90→---
    91→
    92→### `TestCase`
    93→A single input/output pair used to judge a submission.
    94→
    95→| Property | Type | Notes |
    96→|---|---|---|
    97→| Id | int | |
    98→| InputArgs | string | required — serialized input (e.g. JSON array) |
    99→| ExpectedOutput | string | required |
   100→| IsSample | bool | true = shown to user; false = hidden judge-only |
   101→| OrderIndex | int | display/execution order |
   102→| Points | int | partial scoring weight |
   103→| Problem | Problem | required — owning problem |
   104→
   105→---
   106→
   107→### `Submission`
   108→One attempt by a user to solve a problem.
   109→
   110→| Property | Type | Notes |
   111→|---|---|---|
   112→| Id | int | |
   113→| SourceCode | string | required |
   114→| Language | string | required — e.g. "cpp", "c" |
   115→| Status | SubmissionStatus | |
   116→| SubmittedAt | DateTime | |
   117→| Score | int | 0–100, based on passing test cases |
   118→| WallTimeMs | int | measured execution time |
   119→| PeakMemoryKb | int | measured peak memory |
   120→| User | User | required |
   121→| Problem | Problem | required |
   122→| ExecutionResult | ExecutionResult? | null while status is Pending/Running |
   123→
   124→---
   125→
   126→### `ExecutionResult`
   127→Raw output from the sandbox for a submission.
   128→
   129→| Property | Type | Notes |
   130→|---|---|---|
   131→| Id | int | |
   132→| Stdout | string | required |
   133→| Stderr | string | required |
   134→| ExitCode | int | 0 = clean exit |
   135→| TimedOut | bool | true if wall time exceeded limit |
   136→| MemoryExceeded | bool | true if peak memory exceeded limit |
   137→
   138→---
   139→
   140→### `ProblemSet`
   141→An ordered collection of problems — used for courses or curated playlists.
   142→
   143→| Property | Type | Notes |
   144→|---|---|---|
   145→| Id | int | |
   146→| Title | string | required |
   147→| Description | string | required |
   148→| CreatedAt | DateTime | |
   149→| IsPublic | bool | controls visibility to non-members |
   150→| OrderIndex | int | ordering within the organization |
   151→| Organization | Organization | required — owning org |
   152→| Problems | List\<Problem\> | N-N with Problem |
   153→
   154→---
   155→
   156→## Relationships
   157→
   158→```mermaid
   159→erDiagram
   160→    User {
   161→        int Id
   162→        string Username
   163→        string Email
   164→        string DisplayName
   165→        UserRole Role
   166→        DateTime RegisteredAt
   167→    }
   168→    Organization {
   169→        int Id
   170→        string Name
   171→        string Description
   172→    }
   173→    Problem {
   174→        int Id
   175→        string Title
   176→        string Description
   177→        DifficultyLevel Difficulty
   178→        int TimeLimitMs
   179→        int MemoryLimitKb
   180→        DateTime CreatedAt
   181→        string AuthorUsername
   182→    }
   183→    ProblemSet {
   184→        int Id
   185→        string Title
   186→        string Description
   187→        DateTime CreatedAt
   188→        bool IsPublic
   189→        int OrderIndex
   190→    }
   191→    Tag {
   192→        int Id
   193→        string Name
   194→    }
   195→    TestCase {
   196→        int Id
   197→        string InputArgs
   198→        string ExpectedOutput
   199→        bool IsSample
   200→        int OrderIndex
   201→        int Points
   202→    }
   203→    Submission {
   204→        int Id
   205→        string SourceCode
   206→        string Language
   207→        SubmissionStatus Status
   208→        DateTime SubmittedAt
   209→        int Score
   210→        int WallTimeMs
   211→        int PeakMemoryKb
   212→    }
   213→    ExecutionResult {
   214→        int Id
   215→        string Stdout
   216→        string Stderr
   217→        int ExitCode
   218→        bool TimedOut
   219→        bool MemoryExceeded
   220→    }
   221→
   222→    User             ||--o{ Submission    : "makes"
   223→    User             }o--o{ Organization  : "belongs to"
   224→    Organization     ||--o{ ProblemSet    : "owns"
   225→    ProblemSet       }o--o{ Problem       : "contains"
   226→    Problem          ||--o{ TestCase      : "has"
   227→    Problem          ||--o{ Submission    : "receives"
   228→    Problem          }o--o{ Tag           : "tagged with"
   229→    Submission       ||--o| ExecutionResult : "produces"
   230→```
   231→
   232→Key relationships:
   233→- **1-N**: Problem → TestCases, Problem → Submissions, User → Submissions, Organization → ProblemSets
   234→- **N-N**: Problem ↔ Tag, Problem ↔ ProblemSet, User ↔ Organization
   235→- `ExecutionResult` is nullable on `Submission` — it only exists after the sandbox has finished running
   236→

    28→# Osnove C# jezika
    29→
    30→U ovom će se poglavlju prezentirati osnovni pojmovi i koncepti koji će biti korišteni na kolegiju.
    31→
    32→## Solution i projekti
    33→
    34→U pravilu se svaka ozbiljnija aplikacija dijeli na nekoliko slojeva. Ti slojevi su najčešće prezentacijski sloj, model/business/services sloj i sloj za pristup bazi podataka (Data Access Layer ili kraće DAL sloj). Kod razvoja višeslojnih aplikacija, potrebno je uvesti i nezavisnosti između slojeva u „smjeru prema gore", što znači da prezentacijski sloj može biti ovisan o DAL ili model sloju, ali DAL ili model sloj ne smiju biti ovisni o prezentacijskom sloju. U .NET okruženju se takva raspodjela najelegantnije postiže raspodjelom slojeva na projekte unutar rješenja (solutiona). Po uzoru na tu ideju, napravit ćemo novi projekt i nazvati ga Vjezba.Model. Novi projekt se dodaje odabirom opcije **File → Add → New Project** u izborniku.
    35→
    36→Ako pogledamo solution explorer, novi projekt je kreiran i automatski mu je dodana nova klasa Class1, koju možemo slobodno obrisati.
    37→
    38→## Klase i objekti, svojstva u klasi
    39→
    40→Nova klasa u projekt se dodaje tako da u solution explorer prozoru, na željenom projektu, odaberemo desni klik i add new item, te pronađemo odgovarajuću stavku iz izbornika.
    41→
    42→<aside>
    43→⚠️
    44→
    45→**Važna napomena**: pri dodavanju nove klase njena vidljivost je automatski postavljena na „internal", te da bi klasa bila vidljiva u drugim projektima potrebno ju je definirati kao „public".
    46→
    47→</aside>
    48→
    49→Primjerice, ukoliko dodamo klasu **Fakultet** u projekt Vjezba.Model, moramo dodati ključnu riječ „public":
    50→
    51→```csharp
    52→namespace Vjezba.Model
    53→{
    54→    public class Fakultet
    55→    {
    56→    }
    57→}
    58→```
    59→
    60→### Odnos klasa
    61→
    62→Klase mogu implementirati nasljeđivanje, što znači da izvedena klasa nasljeđuje svojstva i funkcije iz bazne klase. Klase također mogu biti u odnosu u kojem neka klasa sadrži niz referenci na objekte druge klase, najčešće u obliku liste. U nastavku je dan primjer, gdje klasa A nasljeđuje klasu B (B je bazna klasa), dok klasa A također ima u sebi listu referenci na objekte klase C (tzv. odnos 1-N).
    63→
    64→```csharp
    65→public class B
    66→{
    67→}
    68→
    69→public class A : B
    70→{
    71→    private List<C> _objektiKlaseC;
    72→}
    73→
    74→public class C
    75→{
    76→}
    77→```
    78→
    79→### Svojstva i polja, konstruktori i metode
    80→
    81→Kada govorimo o informacijama koje su sadržane u klasama, najčešće koristimo termine kao što su svojstva (**properties**) i rijeđe polja (**fields**). Polje (član) je doslovno varijabla koja je sadržana unutar neke klase, i kad se kreira instanca neke klase, moguće je koristiti to polje kao i svaku drugu varijablu. Iz gornjeg isječka koda, član `_objektiKlaseC` je privatna varijabla, koja je tipa `List<C>`. U pravilu, članovi bi uvijek trebali biti privatni, što znači da se tim članovima ne može pristupiti nigdje izvan klase u kojoj se nalaze. Svojstvo, s druge strane, možemo promatrati kao getter i setter funkciju, samo napisanu malo drukčijom sintaksom nego što je to u C++ ili Java jeziku:
    82→
    83→```csharp
    84→public class Osoba
    85→{
    86→    private string _adresa;
    87→    public string Adresa
    88→    {
    89→        get
    90→        {
    91→            return this._adresa;
    92→        }
    93→        set
    94→        {
    95→            this._adresa = value;
    96→        }
    97→    }
    98→}
    99→```

Perfect! Now let me create a comprehensive summary. I have enough information to provide a thorough overview of the project.

## Comprehensive Project Overview

Based on my exploration of the WebIde project, here is a detailed analysis across all five dimensions you requested:

---

### 1. Overall Project Structure (Solution & Projects)

**Solution File**: `/Users/fbeleta/Documents/WebIde/Web_ide/WebIde.slnx`

The solution consists of two .NET projects:
- **WebIde.Model** (.NET 10 class library) - Pure domain model with no framework dependencies
- **WebIde.Console** (.NET 10 console app) - Lab-1 assignment that exercises the model with LINQ queries and async patterns

**Additional Components**:
- **WebIde.Frontend** - React 19 + TypeScript + Vite frontend (not included in the solution file yet, separate directory)
- **docs/** - Architecture and domain model documentation
- **lab-1/** - AI agent usage logs (course requirement)
- **design-files/** - UI/UX design mockups for various pages

---

### 2. Database-Related Code

**Current Status**: NO database code exists yet. This is entirely in the planning phase.

**Domain Model Classes** (in WebIde.Model):

| Class | Properties | Notes |
|-------|-----------|-------|
| **User** | Id, Username, Email, DisplayName, Role (enum), RegisteredAt, Submissions (List), Organizations (List) | Represents registered users with role-based access |
| **Problem** | Id, Title, Description, Difficulty, TimeLimitMs, MemoryLimitKb, CreatedAt, AuthorUsername, TestCases, Tags, Submissions | Coding problem definition with constraints |
| **Submission** | Id, SourceCode, Language, Status (enum), SubmittedAt, Score, WallTimeMs, PeakMemoryKb, User, Problem, ExecutionResult | User's attempt to solve a problem |
| **TestCase** | Id, InputArgs, ExpectedOutput, IsSample, OrderIndex, Points, Problem | Input/output pair for problem validation |
| **ExecutionResult** | Id, Stdout, Stderr, ExitCode, TimedOut, MemoryExceeded | Raw output from sandbox execution |
| **Organization** | Id, Name, Description, Members (List), ProblemSets (List) | Group of users (course/team/institution) |
| **ProblemSet** | Id, Title, Description, CreatedAt, IsPublic, OrderIndex, Organization, Problems | Ordered collection of problems |
| **Tag** | Id, Name, Problems (List) | Problem category labels (Arrays, Hash Map, etc.) |

**Enums**:
- `DifficultyLevel` - Easy, Medium, Hard
- `SubmissionStatus` - Pending → Running → {Accepted, WrongAnswer, TimeLimitExceeded, MemoryLimitExceeded, CompileError}
- `UserRole` - Admin, Instructor, Student

**Key Relationships**:
- **1-N**: User → Submissions, Problem → TestCases/Submissions, Organization → ProblemSets
- **N-N**: User ↔ Organization, Problem ↔ Tag, Problem ↔ ProblemSet
- `ExecutionResult` is nullable on `Submission` (only exists after sandbox execution completes)

**No DbContext, migrations, or connection strings** - The model is pure C# with no EF Core dependencies. It's designed to be referenced by any layer (future backend, tests, etc.).

---

### 3. Configuration Files

**Frontend Configuration**:
- **package.json** - React 19.2.4, TypeScript, Vite, TailwindCSS 3.4.19, React Router v7, ESLint
- **tsconfig.json** - TypeScript configuration (strict mode enabled)
- **vite.config.ts** - Minimal Vite config with React plugin
- **tailwind.config.js** - Design system tokens configured (surface colors, typography)
- **postcss.config.js** - PostCSS with Tailwind
- **eslint.config.js** - ESLint configuration

**Project Files**:
- **WebIde.Model.csproj** - Basic .NET 10 class library (no NuGet dependencies)
- **WebIde.Console.csproj** - Console app referencing WebIde.Model

**Backend Configuration**: NOT YET CREATED
- No appsettings.json
- No docker-compose.yml  
- No Dockerfile
- No database connection strings

**Local Settings**:
- `.claude/settings.local.json` - Claude Code settings

---

### 4. Project Purpose & Domain

**WebIde** is a **self-hosted competitive coding platform** similar to LeetCode, designed as a learning project.

**Core Functionality**:
- Users browse and select coding problems
- Write code in a browser-based editor (Monaco Editor planned)
- Submit code for execution in an isolated sandbox
- Get real-time execution results streamed back via SignalR
- Track submissions, scores, and leaderboards

**Planned Stack**:
```
Frontend:        React + TypeScript (Vite dev, Nginx prod)
Backend:         ASP.NET MVC (.NET 10) with EF Core
Real-time:       SignalR for streaming execution output
Database:        PostgreSQL
Cache/Sessions:  Redis
Code Execution:  Isolated Docker sandbox
Reverse Proxy:   Nginx routing (/app/* → frontend, /api/* → backend)
```

**Scaling Plan**:
- Stage 1 (~100 users): Single VPS, 2 sandbox runners
- Stage 2 (~500 users): Dedicated worker node, managed PostgreSQL
- Stage 3+ (5000+ users): Kubernetes with auto-scaling worker pools

---

### 5. TODO/Placeholder Code & Planned Functionality

**Lab-1 (Completed)**: 
- Domain model with 8 classes, all required enums and relationships
- Sample data: 3 problems, 3 users, 1 organization, 8 submissions
- LINQ queries demonstrating filtering, ordering, grouping, projections
- Async/await demo with parallel code execution simulation

**Remaining TODOs (from architecture docs & codebase)**:

**Backend Layer** (Not started):
- ASP.NET MVC project not created
- DbContext not created
- EF Core migrations not configured
- REST API endpoints not implemented
- Business logic layer not implemented
- SignalR hub for real-time output not implemented

**Frontend Layer** (Wireframes complete, implementation started):
- **CodeEditor.tsx** - Has hardcoded problem data, no backend integration
- **ProblemLibrary.tsx** - Shows 6 hardcoded problems, no API calls
- **SubmissionResults.tsx** - Stubbed
- **UserProfile.tsx** - Stubbed
- No actual API client/fetch calls
- No authentication/login flow implemented
- No real-time WebSocket connection (SignalR)

**Infrastructure**:
- Docker setup for sandbox execution not configured
- PostgreSQL connection not configured
- Redis setup not configured
- Nginx reverse proxy configuration not created
- CI/CD pipeline not configured

**Sandbox Execution** (Planned):
- Code submission runner
- Isolated execution environment
- Resource limits (CPU, memory, time)
- Result collection and streaming

**Database**:
- EF Core DbContext mapping entities to PostgreSQL
- Migrations strategy
- Repository pattern implementation
- Connection pooling configuration

**Features**:
- User authentication (registration, login, password reset)
- Problem search and filtering
- Leaderboards and user statistics
- Code execution history
- Organization management
- Problem authoring/administration

**Frontend Polish**:
- Responsive design refinement
- Material Design icons integration (placeholders exist)
- Dark mode implementation
- Accessibility improvements

---

1→# Architecture
     2→
     3→## Overview
     4→
     5→WebIde is a self-hosted competitive coding platform. Users write code in a browser-based editor, submit it, and get results streamed back in real time. The system separates concerns into distinct layers: presentation, business logic, and code execution.
     6→
     7→## Current State
     8→
     9→The domain model layer (`WebIde.Model`) is implemented as a .NET class library. A console app (`WebIde.Console`) exercises the model with LINQ queries and async patterns. No web layer exists yet.
    10→
    11→## Target Architecture
    12→
    13→```
    14→Browser
    15→  └── React/TypeScript SPA
    16→        │
    17→        │ HTTP /api/*
    18→        ▼
    19→  ASP.NET MVC (.NET 10)          ← controllers, services, EF Core
    20→        │
    21→        ├── PostgreSQL            ← persistent storage
    22→        ├── Redis                 ← sessions, caching
    23→        └── Sandbox Worker        ← isolated code execution (Docker)
    24→              │
    25→              │ SignalR
    26→              ▼
    27→        Browser (real-time execution output stream)
    28→```
    29→
    30→Nginx sits in front of everything as a reverse proxy:
    31→- `/app/*` → React frontend (Nginx static in prod, Vite dev server in dev)
    32→- `/api/*` → ASP.NET MVC backend
    33→
    34→## Layers
    35→
    36→### WebIde.Model
    37→Pure C# class library. No dependencies on any framework. Contains:
    38→- Domain entity classes (Problem, Submission, User, etc.)
    39→- Enums (DifficultyLevel, SubmissionStatus, UserRole)
    40→
    41→This library is referenced by any layer that needs to work with the domain — backend API, tests, etc.
    42→
    43→### Backend (planned)
    44→ASP.NET MVC (.NET 10). Responsibilities:
    45→- REST API for the frontend
    46→- Business logic (submission scoring, leaderboard updates)
    47→- EF Core ORM talking to PostgreSQL
    48→- SignalR hub for streaming execution output
    49→
    50→### Frontend (planned)
    51→React + TypeScript, bundled with Vite. Responsibilities:
    52→- Code editor (Monaco Editor)
    53→- Problem browser, submission history, leaderboard
    54→- SignalR client for real-time output
    55→
    56→### Sandbox (planned)
    57→Isolated execution environment for untrusted code. Each submission gets:
    58→- A container with strict CPU, memory, and time limits
    59→- No network access
    60→- Results (stdout, stderr, exit code, wall time, peak memory) written back to the backend
    61→
    62→## Scaling Plan
    63→
    64→| Stage | Users | Infrastructure |
    65→|---|---|---|
    66→| 1 | ~100 | Single VPS (4 vCPU / 8 GB), 2 sandbox runners |
    67→| 2 | ~500 | App node + dedicated worker node, managed PostgreSQL |
    68→| 3+ | 5 000+ | Kubernetes, auto-scaling worker pools, read replicas |
    69→
    70→The bottleneck at scale is the sandbox, not the API or database. C/C++ submissions take ~1s compile + ~2s run, so sandbox runners need dedicated CPU for deterministic timing.
    71→

1→# Domain Model
     2→
     3→All classes live in `WebIde.Model`. The model is a pure class library with no framework dependencies — it can be referenced by any layer.
     4→
     5→## Enums
     6→
     7→### `DifficultyLevel`
     8→Classifies problem difficulty.
     9→```
    10→Easy | Medium | Hard
    11→```
    12→
    13→### `SubmissionStatus`
    14→Tracks the lifecycle of a code submission.
    15→```
    16→Pending → Running → Accepted
    17→                  → WrongAnswer
    18→                  → TimeLimitExceeded
    19→                  → MemoryLimitExceeded
    20→                  → CompileError
    21→```
    22→
    23→### `UserRole`
    24→Controls what a user can do on the platform.
    25→```
    26→Admin | Instructor | Student
    27→```
    28→
    29→---
    30→
    31→## Classes
    32→
    33→### `User`
    34→A registered platform user.
    35→
    36→| Property | Type | Notes |
    37→|---|---|---|
    38→| Id | int | |
    39→| Username | string | required |
    40→| Email | string | required |
    41→| DisplayName | string | required |
    42→| Role | UserRole | |
    43→| RegisteredAt | DateTime | |
    44→| Submissions | List\<Submission\> | 1-N: user has many submissions |
    45→| Organizations | List\<Organization\> | N-N: user belongs to many orgs |
    46→
    47→---
    48→
    49→### `Organization`
    50→A group of users — represents a course, team, or institution.
    51→
    52→| Property | Type | Notes |
    53→|---|---|---|
    54→| Id | int | |
    55→| Name | string | required |
    56→| Description | string | required |
    57→| Members | List\<User\> | N-N with User |
    58→| ProblemSets | List\<ProblemSet\> | 1-N: org owns many problem sets |
    59→
    60→---
    61→
    62→### `Problem`
    63→A coding problem with a description and constraints.
    64→
    65→| Property | Type | Notes |
    66→|---|---|---|
    67→| Id | int | |
    68→| Title | string | required |
    69→| Description | string | required |
    70→| Difficulty | DifficultyLevel | |
    71→| TimeLimitMs | int | execution time cap per submission |
    72→| MemoryLimitKb | int | memory cap per submission |
    73→| CreatedAt | DateTime | |
    74→| AuthorUsername | string | required |
    75→| TestCases | List\<TestCase\> | 1-N: problem has many test cases |
    76→| Tags | List\<Tag\> | N-N: problem has many tags |
    77→| Submissions | List\<Submission\> | 1-N: problem has many submissions |
    78→
    79→---
    80→
    81→### `Tag`
    82→A label for categorizing problems (e.g. "Arrays", "Binary Search").
    83→
    84→| Property | Type | Notes |
    85→|---|---|---|
    86→| Id | int | |
    87→| Name | string | required |
    88→| Problems | List\<Problem\> | N-N with Problem |
    89→
    90→---
    91→
    92→### `TestCase`
    93→A single input/output pair used to judge a submission.
    94→
    95→| Property | Type | Notes |
    96→|---|---|---|
    97→| Id | int | |
    98→| InputArgs | string | required — serialized input (e.g. JSON array) |
    99→| ExpectedOutput | string | required |
   100→| IsSample | bool | true = shown to user; false = hidden judge-only |
   101→| OrderIndex | int | display/execution order |
   102→| Points | int | partial scoring weight |
   103→| Problem | Problem | required — owning problem |
   104→
   105→---
   106→
   107→### `Submission`
   108→One attempt by a user to solve a problem.
   109→
   110→| Property | Type | Notes |
   111→|---|---|---|
   112→| Id | int | |
   113→| SourceCode | string | required |
   114→| Language | string | required — e.g. "cpp", "c" |
   115→| Status | SubmissionStatus | |
   116→| SubmittedAt | DateTime | |
   117→| Score | int | 0–100, based on passing test cases |

   120→| User | User | required |

   124→---
   125→
   126→### `ExecutionResult`
   127→Raw output from the sandbox for a submission.
   128→
   129→| Property | Type | Notes |
   130→|---|---|---|
   131→| Id | int | |
   132→| Stdout | string | required |
   133→| Stderr | string | required |
   134→| ExitCode | int | 0 = clean exit |
   135→| TimedOut | bool | true if wall time exceeded limit |
   136→| MemoryExceeded | bool | true if peak memory exceeded limit |
   137→
   138→---
   139→
   140→### `ProblemSet`
   141→An ordered collection of problems — used for courses or curated playlists.
   142→
   143→| Property | Type | Notes |
   144→|---|---|---|
   145→| Id | int | |
   146→| Title | string | required |
   147→| Description | string | required |
   148→| CreatedAt | DateTime | |
   149→| IsPublic | bool | controls visibility to non-members |
   150→| OrderIndex | int | ordering within the organization |
   151→| Organization | Organization | required — owning org |
   152→| Problems | List\<Problem\> | N-N with Problem |

   156→## Relationships
   157→
   158→```mermaid
   159→erDiagram
   160→    User {
   161→        int Id
   162→        string Username
   163→        string Email
   164→        string DisplayName
   165→        UserRole Role
   166→        DateTime RegisteredAt
   167→    }
   168→    Organization {
   169→        int Id
   170→        string Name
   171→        string Description
   172→    }
   173→    Problem {
   174→        int Id
   175→        string Title
   176→        string Description
   177→        DifficultyLevel Difficulty
   178→        int TimeLimitMs
   179→        int MemoryLimitKb
   180→        DateTime CreatedAt
   181→        string AuthorUsername
   182→    }
   183→    ProblemSet {
   184→        int Id
   185→        string Title
   186→        string Description
   187→        DateTime CreatedAt
   188→        bool IsPublic
   189→        int OrderIndex
   190→    }
   191→    Tag {
   192→        int Id
   193→        string Name
   194→    }
   195→    TestCase {
   196→        int Id
   197→        string InputArgs
   198→        string ExpectedOutput
   199→        bool IsSample
   200→        int OrderIndex
   201→        int Points
   202→    }
   203→    Submission {
   204→        int Id
   205→        string SourceCode
   206→        string Language
   207→        SubmissionStatus Status
   208→        DateTime SubmittedAt
   209→        int Score
   210→        int WallTimeMs

   212→    }
   213→    ExecutionResult {
   214→        int Id

   220→    }
   221→
   222→    User             ||--o{ Submission    : "makes"
   223→    User             }o--o{ Organization  : "belongs to"
   224→    Organization     ||--o{ ProblemSet    : "owns"
   225→    ProblemSet       }o--o{ Problem       : "contains"
   226→    Problem          ||--o{ TestCase      : "has"

1→# Design System Specification: Core Identity & Implementation
     2→
     3→## 1. Overview & Creative North Star: "The Obsidian Monolith"
     4→The creative north star for this design system is **"The Obsidian Monolith."** It represents an elite, technical terminal—an interface that feels carved from a single block of dark matter, where every pixel serves a functional purpose and distractions are surgically removed. 
     5→
     6→We are moving away from the soft, rounded "SaaS" aesthetic of the last decade. Instead, we embrace **Functional Brutalism.** By utilizing sharp 0px corners, high-contrast typography, and an uncompromising dark palette, we create a sense of digital authority. This design system breaks the "template" look through intentional asymmetry: heavy-weighted headers may sit flush-left while data-points are tucked into the far-right margins, creating a sophisticated, editorial-technical layout that feels bespoke.
     7→
     8→---
     9→
    10→## 2. Color Palette & The Tonal Logic
    11→This system is strictly monochromatic with a singular, high-vibrancy "digital" spark. We prohibit any blue, navy, or warm undertones. All greys must be neutral or cool-neutral.
    12→
    13→### Core Tokens
    14→- **Surface (Base):** `#131313` (The void)
    15→- **Surface Container Lowest:** `#0e0e0e` (Recessed areas)
    16→- **Surface Container High:** `#2a2a2a` (Raised platforms)
    17→- **Primary (Accent):** `#4edea3` (Mint Green - The interactive spark)
    18→- **Outline Variant:** `#474747` (For "Ghost Borders")
    19→
    20→### The "No-Line" Rule
    21→Traditional 1px solid borders for sectioning are strictly prohibited. Boundaries must be defined through **Background Color Shifts**. To separate a sidebar from a main feed, use `surface-container-low` against the `surface` background. The eye should perceive the change in depth through the change in value, not a structural line.
    22→
    23→### Surface Hierarchy & Nesting
    24→Treat the UI as a series of physical layers stacked on top of one another.
    25→- **Level 0 (Background):** `surface` (#131313)
    26→- **Level 1 (Sectioning):** `surface-container-low` (#1c1b1b)
    27→- **Level 2 (Active Cards):** `surface-container-highest` (#353534)
    28→- **Interactive:** `surface-tint` (#4edea3)
    29→
    30→---
    31→
    32→## 3. Typography: Technical Editorial
    33→We use **Space Grotesk** across the entire system. Its monospace-inspired architecture provides the "technical" DNA required, while its proportional spacing ensures readability at high-end editorial scales.
    34→
    35→- **Display-LG (3.5rem):** Reserved for singular, high-impact data points or section titles.
    36→- **Headline-SM (1.5rem):** Used for primary navigation headers. Always set to a tighter letter-spacing (-0.02em) to maintain a "dense" technical look.
    37→- **Body-MD (0.875rem):** The workhorse for all data. 
    38→- **Label-SM (0.6875rem):** Used for metadata, timestamps, and micro-copy. Always uppercase with +0.05em letter spacing.
    39→
    40→The hierarchy is built on extreme contrast. Pair a `Display-LG` number with a `Label-SM` caption to create a "data-viz" aesthetic even in standard layouts.
    41→
    42→---
    43→
    44→## 4. Elevation & Depth: Tonal Layering
    45→This system rejects traditional drop shadows. We communicate "lift" through color and transparency.
    46→
    47→- **The Layering Principle:** To create a "floating" modal, do not apply a shadow. Instead, use `surface-container-highest` and wrap it in a 1px "Ghost Border" using `outline-variant` at 20% opacity.
    48→- **Glassmorphism:** For floating overlays (like tooltips or dropdowns), use `surface-container-high` with a 70% opacity and a `backdrop-blur` of 20px. This allows the high-contrast text beneath to "ghost" through, maintaining the monolithic feel.
    49→- **Signature Textures:** For high-priority CTAs, use a subtle linear gradient from `primary` (#ffffff) to `primary-container` (#5fedb0). This provides a "metallic" sheen that feels premium and intentional.
    50→
    51→---
    52→
    53→## 5. Components
    54→
    55→### Buttons
    56→- **Primary:** Background `primary-container` (#5fedb0), Text `on-primary-container` (#000000). Sharp 0px corners.
    57→- **Secondary:** Transparent background, 1px "Ghost Border" (`outline-variant` @ 40%), Text `primary` (#ffffff).
    58→- **Tertiary:** No border, no background. Underline on hover using the `primary` (#4edea3) color at 2px thickness.
    59→
    60→### Input Fields
    61→- **Style:** Never use "boxed" inputs. Use a "Bottom-Border Only" approach or a solid `surface-container-highest` block.
    62→- **State:** On focus, the bottom border transitions to `primary` (#4edea3). Error states use `error` (#ffb4ab) with zero rounding.
    63→
    64→### Cards & Lists
    65→- **Rule:** Forbid divider lines.
    66→- **Implementation:** Separate list items using 8px of vertical white space or a subtle hover state shift to `surface-bright`. Use the "Obsidian" depth: a card should be `surface-container-lowest` when inactive, shifting to `surface-container-high` on hover.
    67→
    68→### Data Monoliths (Special Component)
    69→For key metrics, use a full-width block with a `surface-container-low` background. Place the value in `display-md` and the label in `label-sm` (uppercase) in the top-left corner. This emphasizes the "technical terminal" personality.
    70→
    71→---
    72→
    73→## 6. Do’s and Don’ts
    74→
    75→### Do:
    76→- **Do** use 0px border-radius everywhere. No exceptions.
    77→- **Do** use Mint Green (#4edea3) sparingly. It is a "laser pointer," not a "paint brush."
    78→- **Do** lean into white space. High-end design requires "breathing room" to justify its technical complexity.
    79→- **Do** align elements to a strict baseline grid to reinforce the "Monolith" structure.
    80→
    81→### Don’t:
    82→- **Don’t** use shadows to create depth. Use tonal shifts between greys.
    83→- **Don’t** use icons with rounded terminals. Only use sharp, geometric iconography.
    84→- **Don’t** use any color with a Blue/Navy hex code. If a grey looks "too blue," move it toward the neutral #1A1A1A.
    85→- **Don’t** use center alignment for large blocks of text. Stick to the "Technical Editorial" flush-left grid.

1→# Design System Specification: Architectural Precision
     2→
     3→## 1. Overview & Creative North Star: "The Obsidian Monolith"
     4→This design system is a study in restrained power and technical permanence. It rejects the "softness" of modern consumer interfaces in favor of a brutalist, architectural aesthetic. The Creative North Star is **"The Digital Ledger"**—an interface that feels carved from stone, possessing the unwavering precision of a high-end technical instrument.
     5→
     6→To move beyond generic dark modes, this system utilizes a "Hard-Edge" philosophy. We ignore the standard practice of rounded corners and soft shadows. Instead, we embrace sharp 0px radii and intentional tonal layering. We move away from traditional grids by using extreme typographic scale shifts and "ghost" boundaries to create an interface that feels like a singular, solid entity rather than a collection of floating widgets.
     7→
     8→---
     9→
    10→## 2. Colors: The Void and the Signal
    11→The palette is strictly monochromatic, rooted in absolute black. This provides a high-density foundation where the only "life" comes from the Mint Green signal.
    12→
    13→| Token | Value | Role / Intent |
    14→| :--- | :--- | :--- |
    15→| `surface` | #131313 | The primary canvas; a deep, neutral obsidian. |
    16→| `surface_container_lowest` | #0e0e0e | Recessed areas, used for background "wells" or input fields. |
    17→| `surface_container_high` | #2a2a2a | Elevated modules; used to lift content off the primary surface. |
    18→| `primary` | #ffffff | Essential content and high-contrast typography. |
    19→| `surface_tint` | #4edea3 | **The Signal.** Mint Green. Used exclusively for success and primary calls to action. |
    20→| `outline_variant` | #474747 | The "Ghost Border." The only permissible stroke for structural definition. |
    21→
    22→### The "No-Line" Rule
    23→Designers are prohibited from using 1px solid white or high-contrast borders to section content. Boundaries must be defined through **Background Shift**. To separate a sidebar from a main view, transition from `surface` (#131313) to `surface_container_low` (#1c1b1b). Use the technical "thin, subtle border" (#2a2a2a) only when absolute containment is required for interactive elements.
    24→
    25→### Signature Textures
    26→While the system is brutalist, it is not flat. Use a subtle **Linear Gradient** on primary action buttons, transitioning from `surface_tint` (#4edea3) to `primary_fixed` (#006c4a) at a 135-degree angle. This provides a "machined" look that mimics technical hardware.
    27→
    28→---
    29→
    30→## 3. Typography: Architectural Clarity
    31→We use **Space Grotesk** across the entire system. Its geometric construction and idiosyncratic "g" and "y" provide the necessary technical "soul."
    32→
    33→*   **Display (L/M/S):** Used for data visualization headers or hero sections. These should be set with `-0.04em` letter spacing to feel compact and "heavy."
    34→*   **Headline & Title:** The "Architectural Labels." Use Sentence case only. Never use All-Caps for headlines; let the weight and scale carry the authority.
    35→*   **Body (L/M/S):** Optimized for technical legibility. Line height is set generously (1.5) to balance the density of the black background.
    36→*   **Labels:** Small, precise metadata. These are the "serial numbers" of your UI.
    37→
    38→---
    39→
    40→## 4. Elevation & Depth: Tonal Layering
    41→In this system, "Up" does not mean "Closer to the light." It means "Higher Contrast."
    42→
    43→*   **The Layering Principle:** Depth is achieved by stacking surface tiers. A `surface_container_highest` (#353534) card placed on a `surface` (#131313) background creates a sharp, physical lift.
    44→*   **Zero Shadows:** Traditional drop shadows are forbidden. To simulate a "floating" state (like a modal), use a 1px border of `outline_variant` and increase the background contrast to `surface_bright` (#3a3939).
    45→*   **The "Ghost Border" Fallback:** When a component requires a boundary (e.g., a text input), use the #2a2a2a border specified in the brief. It must feel like a hairline trigger—precise and barely there.
    46→*   **Glassmorphism:** For overlays or navigation bars, use `surface` (#131313) at 80% opacity with a `20px` backdrop blur. This ensures the "Obsidian" feel remains consistent as the user scrolls.
    47→
    48→---
    49→
    50→## 5. Components: The Technical Kit
    51→
    52→### Buttons
    53→*   **Primary:** Fill `surface_tint` (#4edea3), text `on_primary` (#002114). Sharp 0px corners.
    54→*   **Secondary:** Ghost style. 1px border of `outline` (#919191), text `primary` (#ffffff).
    55→*   **Tertiary:** Text only, `primary` (#ffffff) with an underline visible only on hover.
    56→
    57→### Input Fields
    58→*   **Style:** Background `surface_container_lowest` (#0e0e0e), bottom-border only (1px, #2a2a2a). 
    59→*   **State:** On focus, the bottom border transforms to `surface_tint` (#4edea3).
    60→
    61→### Cards & Lists
    62→*   **Rule:** Forbid divider lines. Use `24px` or `32px` vertical gaps to separate list items.
    63→*   **Nesting:** Nested data should sit on a slightly lighter surface (`surface_container_low`) to indicate hierarchy.
    64→
    65→### The "Monolith" Data Widget (Custom Component)
    66→For technical readouts, use a `surface_container_highest` block with a 2px left-accent border of `surface_tint`. This acts as a high-visibility container for critical system status or primary metrics.
    67→
    68→---
    69→
    70→## 6. Do’s and Don’ts
    71→
    72→### Do:
    73→*   **Embrace the Void:** Use large areas of `#000000` to create a sense of premium vastness.
    74→*   **Align to the Edge:** Use a strict column grid, but feel free to break the "rows" to create an asymmetric, editorial feel.
    75→*   **Check Contrast:** Ensure all `on_surface_variant` text (#c6c6c6) meets WCAG AA standards against the dark backgrounds.
    76→
    77→### Don't:
    78→*   **No Rounded Corners:** Any radius above `0px` is a violation of the system's architectural integrity.
    79→*   **No Blue Tones:** Absolutely avoid any navy, slate, or cool-grey tones that aren't derived from the obsidian palette.
    80→*   **No Heavy Shadows:** Do not use `box-shadow`. If an element needs to pop, use a tonal shift or a hairline border (#2a2a2a).
    81→*   **No Color Bloat:** If a color isn't Black, Grey, White, or Mint Green, it does not belong in this system.

1→# Design System Document: Technical Precision & Tonal Depth
     2→
     3→## 1. Overview & Creative North Star: "The Monolith"
     4→This design system is built for the high-end developer environment, where focus is the ultimate currency. Our Creative North Star is **"The Monolith"**—a philosophy of architectural solidity, brutalist precision, and technical authority. 
     5→
     6→Unlike "friendly" consumer apps, this system rejects rounded softness and the "rainbow" palette of traditional IDEs. It embraces **intentional asymmetry**, high-contrast editorial typography, and a "subtractive" aesthetic. We do not use lines to define space; we use light and shadow—the digital equivalent of a high-end, matte-black workstation.
     7→
     8→---
     9→
    10→## 2. Colors & Tonal Architecture
    11→The palette is rooted in an ultra-dark spectrum. We avoid a "pure black" (#000000) base to prevent eye fatigue, opting instead for a deep obsidian sequence.
    12→
    13→### The "No-Line" Rule
    14→**Explicit Instruction:** Designers are prohibited from using 1px solid borders for sectioning or layout containment. Boundaries must be defined solely through background color shifts or tonal transitions.
    15→*   **Good:** A `surface_container_low` sidebar sitting against a `surface` editor.
    16→*   **Bad:** Using a `#3c495b` line to separate the file tree from the code view.
    17→
    18→### Surface Hierarchy & Nesting
    19→Treat the UI as a series of physical layers. Use the following tiers to create "nested" depth:
    20→- **Base Layer (`surface`):** The primary editor background.
    21→- **Sunken Layer (`surface_container_low`):** For utility panels (Terminal, File Tree).
    22→- **Elevated Layer (`surface_container_high` / `highest`):** For floating menus, modals, or active overlays.
    23→
    24→### The "Glass & Gradient" Rule
    25→To elevate the experience from "standard tool" to "luxury hardware," use **Glassmorphism** for floating elements (e.g., Command Palettes). 
    26→*   **Implementation:** Use `surface_variant` at 60% opacity with a `24px` backdrop-blur. 
    27→*   **CTAs:** Primary buttons should use a subtle vertical gradient: `primary` (#4edea3) to `primary_dim` (#3cd096) to provide a "machined" look that flat hex codes cannot replicate.
    28→
    29→---
    30→
    31→## 3. Typography: Technical Editorial
    32→We pair a technical, geometric display face with a workhorse sans-serif for maximum legibility.
    33→
    34→- **Space Grotesk (Headings/Display):** Used for "Status" and "Context." It conveys a modern, high-tech engineering feel. Use wide letter-spacing (`0.05em`) for `label-md` to mimic blueprint annotations.
    35→- **Inter (UI/Code):** The engine of the system. High x-height and neutral character for long-term readability in high-density environments.
    36→
    37→**Hierarchy Note:** Use dramatic scale shifts. A `display-lg` headline should feel massive and authoritative, while UI labels remain tiny, sharp, and desaturated (`on_surface_variant`).
    38→
    39→---
    40→
    41→## 4. Elevation & Depth: Tonal Layering
    42→We achieve hierarchy by "stacking" surfaces rather than applying drop shadows.
    43→
    44→- **The Layering Principle:** Place a `surface_container_lowest` card on a `surface_container_low` section. This creates a soft, natural "lift."
    45→- **Ambient Shadows:** For floating modals, use a "Shadow as Atmosphere."
    46→    - **Specs:** `0px 24px 48px`, 6% opacity, using a tinted shadow color based on `surface_container_lowest` rather than pure black.
    47→- **The "Ghost Border" Fallback:** If a border is required for accessibility (e.g., input fields), use the `outline_variant` token at **15% opacity**. Never use 100% opaque borders.
    48→
    49→---
    50→
    51→## 5. Components
    52→All components must adhere to the **Zero-Radius Rule**: `0px` corner radius across all elements to maintain a precise, "pro-tool" silhouette.
    53→
    54→### Buttons
    55→*   **Primary:** Gradient fill (`primary` to `primary_dim`), `on_primary` text. No border.
    56→*   **Secondary:** No fill. `Ghost Border` (15% opacity `outline_variant`). Text in `primary`.
    57→*   **Tertiary:** Ghost style. No fill, no border. Text in `on_surface_variant`. On hover, shift background to `surface_container_high`.
    58→
    59→### Input Fields
    60→*   **Default:** `surface_container_low` background with a bottom-only `outline_variant` (20% opacity). 
    61→*   **Focus:** The bottom border transforms into a 2px `primary` line. No "glow" or outer rings.
    62→
    63→### Cards & Lists
    64→*   **Constraint:** Forbid the use of divider lines. 
    65→*   **Solution:** Use `16px` or `24px` of vertical whitespace. If separation is visually impossible, use a subtle background shift to `surface_container_highest` for the active list item.
    66→
    67→### IDE-Specific Components
    68→*   **Gutter/Line Numbers:** Text color `on_tertiary_container`. Active line number highlighted in `primary`.
    69→*   **Breadcrumbs:** Use `label-sm` in `on_surface_variant`. Separate with a simple `/` or space, not an icon.
    70→*   **Status Bar:** High-contrast `primary_container` background with `on_primary_container` text for the active branch/project.
    71→
    72→---
    73→
    74→## 6. Do’s and Don’ts
    75→
    76→### Do:
    77→*   **Embrace High-Quality Whitespace:** Use space as a structural element. 
    78→*   **Use Mono-Tonal Status:** Use `error_container` (deep burgundy/charcoal) for errors rather than bright red. Only the icon or a small indicator should use the `error` (#ee7d77) token.
    79→*   **Asymmetry:** Align meta-data (like file size or git status) to the far right, leaving significant "breathing room" between the label and the data.
    80→
    81→### Don’t:
    82→*   **Don't use Rounded Corners:** Any radius above 0px violates the system's professional integrity.
    83→*   **Don't use "Rainbow" Syntax:** Avoid a dozen different colors for code. Use variations of `primary`, `secondary`, and `tertiary` to create a cohesive, focused coding environment.
    84→*   **Don't use Heavy Borders:** If you feel a border is needed, try adjusting the background tone of the container first.

## ARCHITECTURE & TECH STACK

**Current State:**
- Domain model layer (`WebIde.Model`) implemented as .NET class library with no framework dependencies
- Console app (`WebIde.Console`) exercises the model with LINQ queries and async patterns
- No web layer exists yet

**Target Architecture:**
```
Browser (React/TypeScript SPA)
    ↓ HTTP /api/*
ASP.NET MVC (.NET 10)
    ├── PostgreSQL (persistent storage)
    ├── Redis (sessions, caching)
    └── Sandbox Worker (isolated code execution via Docker)
        ↓ SignalR
    Browser (real-time execution output)
```

**Key Tech Stack Decisions:**
- **Backend**: ASP.NET MVC (.NET 10) with EF Core ORM
- **Frontend**: React + TypeScript with Vite bundler, Monaco Editor for code editing
- **Database**: PostgreSQL
- **Caching**: Redis
- **Real-time**: SignalR for streaming execution output
- **Execution**: Docker-based sandbox with CPU, memory, and time limits
- **Reverse Proxy**: Nginx routing `/app/*` → frontend, `/api/*` → backend

**Scaling Plan:**
- Stage 1: ~100 users, single VPS (4 vCPU / 8 GB), 2 sandbox runners
- Stage 2: ~500 users, app node + dedicated worker node, managed PostgreSQL
- Stage 3: 5,000+ users, Kubernetes with auto-scaling worker pools and read replicas

- `/components/layout/AppLayout.tsx` - Main container with Outlet
- `/components/layout/TopBar.tsx` - Header with nav links & user menu
- `/components/layout/SideNav.tsx` - Desktop sidebar navigation
- `/components/layout/MobileNav.tsx` - Mobile bottom nav

### Design System Implementation

**Tech Stack:**
- React 19.2.4
- TypeScript 5.9.3
- Tailwind CSS 3.4.19 (custom design tokens in config)
- React Router DOM 7.14.0
- Vite 8.0.1
- Material Symbols Outlined icons (via CDN classes)

**Design Tokens** (from config):
- **Primary:** `#4edea3` (mint green) — interactive, CTAs, highlights
- **Secondary:** `#7bd0ff` (cyan)
- **Tertiary:** `#ffb95f` (amber)
- **Error:** `#ffb4ab` (light red)
- **Surface colors:** Deep navy hierarchy (`surface`, `surface-container-low`, `surface-container`, `surface-container-high`, `surface-container-highest`)
- **Typography:** Space Grotesk (headlines), Inter (body), Fira Code (monospace code)
- **Border strategy:** No solid lines — "ghost borders" using `outline-variant` at reduced opacity; separation via background color shifts
- **Spacing:** Grid-based, generous whitespace

**Key Design Patterns:**
- Gradient CTAs: `bg-gradient-to-r from-primary to-on-primary-container`
- Hover effects: border/background tonal shifts, opacity changes
- No rounded corners on buttons/inputs (sharp 0px)
- Backdrop blur for overlays/modals
- Icons from Material Symbols with `icon-fill` class for filled variants

### Hardcoded Mock Data Locations

| Page | Mock Data | Location |
|------|-----------|----------|
| ProblemLibrary | 6 problems array | Lines 14-21 |
| CodeEditor | 15 code lines (JSX) | Lines 6-22; test cases array line 24 |
| SubmissionResults | 5 test cases | Lines 3-9; code string lines 11-25 |
| UserProfile | Activity array (84 items) | Lines 1-4; orgs array lines 6-21; teams array lines 23-36 |

### Design Files Reference

**Design folders in `design-files/`:**
- `monolith_obsidian_1/` & `monolith_obsidian_2/` — Initial design system specs (DESIGN.md) outlining "Obsidian Monolith" brutalist aesthetic
- `synthetica_code/` — Final design spec (DESIGN.md) with refined "Monolith" philosophy
- Multiple screen mockups (desktop/mobile finals) in HTML + PNG for each page
- Design specs emphasize: 0px border-radius, no lines (tonal shifts only), high-contrast typography, glass morphism for overlays

---

**Summary:** You have a fully functional frontend scaffold with 4 main pages, mock data embedded throughout, responsive layout with desktop sidebar + mobile bottom nav, and a sophisticated dark-mode design system emphasizing tonal hierarchy and minimalist brutalism. All pages are currently standalone and not connected to backend APIs.

Design a detailed implementation plan for the following task on a .NET 10 project.

## Context

**Project**: WebIde — a LeetCode-style coding platform
**Solution location**: /Users/fbeleta/Documents/WebIde/Web_ide/WebIde.slnx

- `WebIde.Console` (.NET 10 console app) — lab exercise, not relevant

## Domain Model (WebIde.Model)

Entities: User, Organization, Problem, ProblemSet, Submission, TestCase, ExecutionResult, Tag

Relationships:
- 1-N: User → Submissions, Problem → TestCases, Problem → Submissions, Organization → ProblemSets
- N-N: User ↔ Organization, Problem ↔ Tag, Problem ↔ ProblemSet
- 0-1: Submission → ExecutionResult (nullable)

Key properties:
- User: Id(int), Username(string), Email(string), DisplayName(string), Role(UserRole), RegisteredAt(DateTime), Submissions(List<Submission>), Organizations(List<Organization>)
- Problem: Id(int), Title(string), Description(string), Difficulty(DifficultyLevel), TimeLimitMs(int), MemoryLimitKb(int), CreatedAt(DateTime), AuthorUsername(string), TestCases(List<TestCase>), Tags(List<Tag>), Submissions(List<Submission>)
- Submission: Id(int), SourceCode(string), Language(string), Status(SubmissionStatus), SubmittedAt(DateTime), Score(int), WallTimeMs(int), PeakMemoryKb(int), User(User), Problem(Problem), ExecutionResult(ExecutionResult?)
- TestCase: Id(int), InputArgs(string), ExpectedOutput(string), IsSample(bool), OrderIndex(int), Points(int), Problem(Problem)
- ExecutionResult: Id(int), Stdout(string), Stderr(string), ExitCode(int), TimedOut(bool), MemoryExceeded(bool)
- Organization: Id(int), Name(string), Description(string), Members(List<User>), ProblemSets(List<ProblemSet>)
- ProblemSet: Id(int), Title(string), Description(string), CreatedAt(DateTime), IsPublic(bool), OrderIndex(int), Organization(Organization), Problems(List<Problem>)
- Tag: Id(int), Name(string), Problems(List<Problem>)

Enums: UserRole (Admin, Instructor, Student), SubmissionStatus (Pending, Running, Accepted, WrongAnswer, TimeLimitExceeded, MemoryLimitExceeded, CompileError), DifficultyLevel (Easy, Medium, Hard)

## Task

Create the following from scratch:

1. **docker-compose.yml** (in solution root) — PostgreSQL 16 + pgAdmin
2. **WebIde.Api** — new ASP.NET Web API project (.NET 10) that:
   - References WebIde.Model
   - Has EF Core + Npgsql packages
   - Has AppDbContext with full entity configuration (Fluent API, keep model pure)
   - Has connection string in appsettings.json pointing to docker postgres
   - Has ProblemsController with GET /api/problems (with optional difficulty/tag filters), GET /api/problems/{id}, POST /api/problems, PUT /api/problems/{id}, DELETE /api/problems/{id}
   - Has Program.cs wiring up EF Core, cors, controllers
3. **Add WebIde.Api to the .slnx solution file**
4. **Initial EF Core migration** (instructions only — the migration command to run)

## Requirements
- Keep WebIde.Model pure (no EF Core attributes or dependencies in the model project)
- Use Fluent API exclusively in AppDbContext for all entity configurations
- Configure all N-N relationships with explicit join tables
- Store enums as strings (not ints) in PostgreSQL for readability
- Connection string should use the docker-compose service name as host
- CORS should allow the Vite dev server (localhost:5173)
- The API should return 404s with problem details (RFC 7807)

Please provide:
- Exact file paths for every file to create/modify
- Exact NuGet packages with versions
- Full AppDbContext configuration including all entity relationships
- ProblemsController implementation
- Program.cs service registration
- docker-compose.yml contents
- The dotnet CLI commands to create the project, add packages, and run migration

input.content:
# Plan: PostgreSQL Setup + WebIde.Api Backend

## Context
The domain model (WebIde.Model) exists as a pure C# class library. No web layer or database layer has been created yet. This plan creates the ASP.NET Web API project, wires up EF Core with PostgreSQL (via Docker Compose), and implements CRUD endpoints for Problems as the first working backend slice.

---

## Files to Create / Modify

| Action | Path |
|--------|------|
| CREATE | `docker-compose.yml` |
| CREATE | `WebIde.Api/WebIde.Api.csproj` |
| CREATE | `WebIde.Api/Program.cs` |
| CREATE | `WebIde.Api/appsettings.json` |
| CREATE | `WebIde.Api/appsettings.Development.json` |
| CREATE | `WebIde.Api/Data/AppDbContext.cs` |
| CREATE | `WebIde.Api/Controllers/ProblemsController.cs` |
| CREATE | `WebIde.Api/DTOs/ProblemDto.cs` |
| CREATE | `WebIde.Api/DTOs/CreateProblemRequest.cs` |
| CREATE | `WebIde.Api/DTOs/UpdateProblemRequest.cs` |
| MODIFY | `WebIde.slnx` (add Api project) |
| GENERATED | `WebIde.Api/Data/Migrations/` (via dotnet ef) |

---

## Step 1 — Bootstrap CLI commands

```bash
# From solution root
dotnet new webapi -n WebIde.Api -o WebIde.Api --no-openapi
cd WebIde.Api
dotnet add package Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore --version 9.0.4
dotnet add package Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Design --version 9.0.4
dotnet add package Npgsql.EntityFrameworkCore.PostgreSQL --version 9.0.4
dotnet add reference ../WebIde.Model/WebIde.Model.csproj
cd ..
dotnet sln WebIde.slnx add WebIde.Api/WebIde.Api.csproj
```

---

## Step 2 — docker-compose.yml

```yaml
services:
  postgres:
    image: postgres:16
    container_name: webide_postgres
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: webide
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: webide_dev
      POSTGRES_DB: webide
    ports:
      - "5432:5432"
    volumes:
      - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    restart: unless-stopped

  pgadmin:
    image: dpage/pgadmin4:latest
    container_name: webide_pgadmin
    environment:
      PGADMIN_DEFAULT_EMAIL: admin@webide.local
      PGADMIN_DEFAULT_PASSWORD: admin
    ports:
      - "5050:80"
    depends_on:
      - postgres
    restart: unless-stopped

volumes:
  postgres_data:
```

---

## Step 3 — Connection strings

**appsettings.json** (for when the API runs inside Docker — uses Docker DNS `postgres`):
```json
{
  "ConnectionStrings": {
    "DefaultConnection": "Host=postgres;Port=5432;Database=webide;Username=webide;Password=webide_dev"
  },
  "Logging": { "LogLevel": { "Default": "Information", "Microsoft.AspNetCore": "Warning", "Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Database.Command": "Information" } },
  "AllowedHosts": "*"
}
```

**appsettings.Development.json** (for `dotnet run` locally — uses localhost port-forward):
```json
{
  "ConnectionStrings": {
    "DefaultConnection": "Host=localhost;Port=5432;Database=webide;Username=webide;Password=webide_dev"
  },
  "Logging": { "LogLevel": { "Default": "Debug", "Microsoft.AspNetCore": "Information", "Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Database.Command": "Information" } }
}
```

---

## Step 4 — Program.cs

```csharp
using Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore;
using WebIde.Api.Data;

var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args);

builder.Services.AddDbContext<AppDbContext>(options =>
    options.UseNpgsql(
        builder.Configuration.GetConnectionString("DefaultConnection"),
        o => o.MigrationsAssembly("WebIde.Api")
    )
);

builder.Services.AddControllers();

builder.Services.AddCors(options =>
    options.AddPolicy("ViteDev", policy =>
        policy.WithOrigins("http://localhost:5173")
              .AllowAnyHeader()
              .AllowAnyMethod()));

- `/profile` — UserProfile: stats bento, organizations, teams, activity heatmap

- .NET solution (WebIde.Console + WebIde.Model) at root
- Frontend at WebIde.Frontend/

```bash
# From solution root — start postgres + pgAdmin

# Verify postgres is healthy before running migrations

```

## Step 9 — pgAdmin: Connect to the Server

1. Open http://localhost:5050 in a browser
2. Log in with:
   - **Email:** `admin@webide.local`
   - **Password:** `admin`
3. In the left panel, right-click **Servers** → **Register → Server...**
4. **General tab:**
   - Name: `webide_dev`
5. **Connection tab:**
   - Host: `postgres` *(Docker internal hostname — pgAdmin runs inside Docker network)*
   - Port: `5432`
   - Maintenance database: `webide`
   - Username: `webide`
   - Password: `webide_dev`
   - Toggle **Save password**
6. Click **Save** — the `webide` database should appear under Servers → webide_dev → Databases

---

## Step 10 — EF Core Migration

```bash
# Install dotnet-ef if not already present
dotnet tool install --global dotnet-ef

# Verify
dotnet ef --version

# From solution root
dotnet ef migrations add InitialSchema \
    --project WebIde.Api \
    --startup-project WebIde.Api \
    --output-dir Data/Migrations

# Apply to the running postgres container
dotnet ef database update \
    --project WebIde.Api \
    --startup-project WebIde.Api
```

This creates all 8 entity tables + 3 join tables in the `webide` database. Confirm in pgAdmin under **Servers → webide_dev → Databases → webide → Schemas → public → Tables**.

1. `docker-compose up -d` → both containers running

5. `GET /api/problems` → returns `[]`
6. `POST /api/problems` body `{"title":"Two Sum","description":"...","difficulty":"Easy","timeLimitMs":1000,"memoryLimitKb":65536,"authorUsername":"admin","tags":["arrays","hash-map"]}` → 201
7. `GET /api/problems/1` → returns created problem
8. `GET /api/problems?difficulty=Easy` → filters correctly

# Plan: PostgreSQL Setup + WebIde.Api Backend

## Context
The domain model (WebIde.Model) exists as a pure C# class library. No web layer or database layer has been created yet. This plan creates the ASP.NET Web API project, wires up EF Core with PostgreSQL (via Docker Compose), and implements CRUD endpoints for Problems as the first working backend slice.

---

## Files to Create / Modify

| Action | Path |
|--------|------|
| CREATE | `docker-compose.yml` |
| CREATE | `WebIde.Api/WebIde.Api.csproj` |
| CREATE | `WebIde.Api/Program.cs` |
| CREATE | `WebIde.Api/appsettings.json` |
| CREATE | `WebIde.Api/appsettings.Development.json` |
| CREATE | `WebIde.Api/Data/AppDbContext.cs` |
| CREATE | `WebIde.Api/Controllers/ProblemsController.cs` |
| CREATE | `WebIde.Api/DTOs/ProblemDto.cs` |
| CREATE | `WebIde.Api/DTOs/CreateProblemRequest.cs` |
| CREATE | `WebIde.Api/DTOs/UpdateProblemRequest.cs` |
| MODIFY | `WebIde.slnx` (add Api project) |
| GENERATED | `WebIde.Api/Data/Migrations/` (via dotnet ef) |

---

## Step 1 — Bootstrap CLI commands

```bash
# From solution root
dotnet new webapi -n WebIde.Api -o WebIde.Api --no-openapi
cd WebIde.Api
dotnet add package Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore --version 9.0.4
dotnet add package Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Design --version 9.0.4
dotnet add package Npgsql.EntityFrameworkCore.PostgreSQL --version 9.0.4
dotnet add reference ../WebIde.Model/WebIde.Model.csproj
cd ..
dotnet sln WebIde.slnx add WebIde.Api/WebIde.Api.csproj
```

---

## Step 2 — docker-compose.yml

```yaml
services:
  postgres:
    image: postgres:16
    container_name: webide_postgres
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: webide
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: webide_dev
      POSTGRES_DB: webide
    ports:
      - "5432:5432"
    volumes:
      - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    restart: unless-stopped

  pgadmin:
    image: dpage/pgadmin4:latest
    container_name: webide_pgadmin
    environment:
      PGADMIN_DEFAULT_EMAIL: admin@webide.local
      PGADMIN_DEFAULT_PASSWORD: admin
    ports:
      - "5050:80"
    depends_on:
      - postgres
    restart: unless-stopped

volumes:
  postgres_data:
```

---

## Step 3 — Connection strings

**appsettings.json** (for when the API runs inside Docker — uses Docker DNS `postgres`):
```json
{
  "ConnectionStrings": {
    "DefaultConnection": "Host=postgres;Port=5432;Database=webide;Username=webide;Password=webide_dev"
  },
  "Logging": { "LogLevel": { "Default": "Information", "Microsoft.AspNetCore": "Warning", "Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Database.Command": "Information" } },
  "AllowedHosts": "*"
}
```

**appsettings.Development.json** (for `dotnet run` locally — uses localhost port-forward):
```json
{
  "ConnectionStrings": {
    "DefaultConnection": "Host=localhost;Port=5432;Database=webide;Username=webide;Password=webide_dev"
  },
  "Logging": { "LogLevel": { "Default": "Debug", "Microsoft.AspNetCore": "Information", "Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Database.Command": "Information" } }
}
```

---

## Step 4 — Program.cs

```csharp
using Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore;
using WebIde.Api.Data;

var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args);

builder.Services.AddDbContext<AppDbContext>(options =>
    options.UseNpgsql(
        builder.Configuration.GetConnectionString("DefaultConnection"),
        o => o.MigrationsAssembly("WebIde.Api")
    )
);

builder.Services.AddControllers();

builder.Services.AddCors(options =>
    options.AddPolicy("ViteDev", policy =>
        policy.WithOrigins("http://localhost:5173")
              .AllowAnyHeader()
              .AllowAnyMethod()));

builder.Services.AddProblemDetails();

var app = builder.Build();
app.UseExceptionHandler();
app.UseStatusCodePages();
app.UseCors("ViteDev");
app.MapControllers();
app.Run();
```

---

## Step 5 — AppDbContext (Data/AppDbContext.cs)

Full Fluent API configuration — WebIde.Model stays pure (no EF attributes).

Key decisions:
- All enums stored as strings: `.HasConversion<string>()`
- All DateTime columns: `.HasColumnType("timestamptz")`
- FK columns as shadow properties: `.HasForeignKey("column_name")`
- N-N join tables: explicit `.UsingEntity(...)` with named tables
- `Problem.ProblemSets` nav does not exist in the model → use `.WithMany()` (no argument) on the ProblemSet side
- `OnDelete` strategy:
  - TestCase/ProblemSet → Cascade (child data, delete with parent)
  - Submission → User/Problem: Restrict (preserve history)
  - Submission → ExecutionResult: SetNull (nullable reference)

Entities and table names:
- `User` → `users`, `Organization` → `organizations`, `Problem` → `problems`
- `ProblemSet` → `problem_sets`, `Submission` → `submissions`
- `TestCase` → `test_cases`, `ExecutionResult` → `execution_results`, `Tag` → `tags`

Join tables:
- `organization_members` (organization_id, user_id)
- `problem_tags` (problem_id, tag_id)
- `problem_set_problems` (problem_set_id, problem_id)

---

## Step 6 — DTOs

**ProblemDto** — read model (record with Id, Title, Description, Difficulty string, TimeLimitMs, MemoryLimitKb, CreatedAt, AuthorUsername, IReadOnlyList\<string\> Tags)

**CreateProblemRequest** — write model (Title, Description, Difficulty string, TimeLimitMs, MemoryLimitKb, AuthorUsername, List\<string\>? Tags) with `[Required]`/`[Range]` annotations

**UpdateProblemRequest** — same as Create minus AuthorUsername

---

## Step 7 — ProblemsController

Routes: `GET /api/problems?difficulty=&tag=`, `GET /api/problems/{id}`, `POST /api/problems`, `PUT /api/problems/{id}`, `DELETE /api/problems/{id}`

Key patterns:
- All 404s return `ProblemDetails` with `Title` + `Detail` (RFC 7807)
- `ValidationProblem()` for invalid enum values
- Tag upsert on POST/PUT: reuse existing tags by name, create new ones — avoids unique constraint violations
- `problem.Tags.Clear()` on PUT triggers EF to delete old join rows without deleting Tag records

---

## Step 8 — Migration

```bash
# Prerequisites: docker-compose up -d postgres
dotnet tool install --global dotnet-ef   # if not installed

dotnet ef migrations add InitialSchema \
    --project WebIde.Api \
    --startup-project WebIde.Api \
    --output-dir Data/Migrations

dotnet ef database update \
    --project WebIde.Api \
    --startup-project WebIde.Api
```

---

## Verification

1. `docker-compose up -d` → postgres running on localhost:5432
2. `cd WebIde.Api && dotnet run` → API starts on https://localhost:5XXX
3. `GET /api/problems` → returns `[]` (empty, no problems yet)
4. `POST /api/problems` with JSON body → returns 201 with new problem
5. `GET /api/problems/{id}` → returns the created problem
6. `GET /api/problems?difficulty=Easy` → filters correctly
7. `PUT /api/problems/{id}` → updates and returns updated problem
8. `DELETE /api/problems/{id}` → returns 204
9. `GET /api/problems/{deleted_id}` → returns 404 with RFC 7807 body
10. pgAdmin at http://localhost:5050 → confirm tables exist in the `webide` database
